content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                          Date : May 4, 1979

                                                            m-79-141

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Dwight Dickerson

Subject: Repayment of Bank Loans as Gross Receipts

        The staff during its consideration of SB1144 concluded
at its April 16, 1979, opinion request meeting that gross
receipts included the payment of interest on a loan. How-
ever, repayment of the principal is not a gross receipt
within the meaning of Government Code Section 82707(b)(2).

        The staff, after making this determination regarding
gross receipts, went on record opposing SB1144 authored by
Senator Ayala. Senator Ayala's bill would exclude from the
disclosure requirements of the Act the reporting of bank
customers pursuant to 87207(b)(2).


DED:mfa

State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants          Date :April 13, 1979


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Dwight Dickerson

Subject: SB 1144 - Proposed amendment to definition of income


     SB 1144 would exclude from the definition of income an
individual's pro rata share of income of any bank or other
financial institution in which the individual has a 10%
interest or greater. The practical effect of the legislation
is to make transaction between a bank, in which a public
official has a significant ownership interest (10% or greater),
and the bank's customer unreportable. In addition, the bank's
customer would not be a source of income to the official for
disqualification purposes. Presently, the Act provides that
a public official, with a 10% interest or greater in a bank,
must file a bank customer, if the official's pro
rata share of the gross receipts is $10,000 or more.

     It is current Commission policy that deposits (savings
and checking accounts) are not gross receipts and individuals
who only maintain accounts need not be reported. I have
reviewed a sample of bank customers who repay loans to banks
are bank customers. If the filer's pro rata share of the
repayment is $10,000 or more, the customer's name should be
disclosed. I suspect that this advice was the impetus for
this proposed legislation.

DED:nrw

sections.conclusion: The staff during its consideration of SB1144 concluded at its April 16, 1979, opinion request meeting that gross receipts included the payment of interest on a loan. However, repayment of the principal is not a gross receipt within the meaning of Government Code Section 87207(b)(2).
sections.question_synthetic: Whether repayment of bank loan principal constitutes gross receipts requiring disclosure under Government Code Section 87207(b)(2), and whether SB 1144's proposed exclusion for bank customers is appropriate.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff concluded that interest payments on loans constitute gross receipts, but principal repayments do not qualify as gross receipts under the statute. The staff opposed SB 1144 as it would improperly exclude bank customer transactions from disclosure requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document comprises two related memoranda (May 4 and April 13, 1979). No formal Q&C sections with headers, but clear opinion is stated regarding loan principal/interest treatment. Second memo provides context for the staff position on SB 1144.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether repayment of bank loan principal constitutes gross receipts requiring disclosure under Government Code Section 87207(b)(2), and whether SB 1144's proposed exclusion for bank customers is appropriate.

CONCLUSION: The staff during its consideration of SB1144 concluded at its April 16, 1979, opinion request meeting that gross receipts included the payment of interest on a loan. However, repayment of the principal is not a gross receipt within the meaning of Government Code Section 87207(b)(2).
embedding.first_500_words: **Memorandum** To: File Date: May 4, 1979 From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Dwight Dickerson Subject: Repayment of Bank Loans as Gross Receipts The staff during its consideration of SB1144 concluded at its April 16, 1979, opinion request meeting that gross receipts included the payment of interest on a loan. However, repayment of the principal is not a gross receipt within the meaning of Government Code Section 87207(b)(2). The staff, after making this determination regarding gross receipts, went on record opposing SB1144 authored by Senator Ayala. Senator Ayala's bill would exclude from the disclosure requirements of the Act the reporting of bank customers pursuant to 87207(b)(2). DED:mfa **State of California** **Memorandum** To: Opinion Request Meeting Participants Date: April 13, 1979 From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Dwight Dickerson Subject: SB 1144 - Proposed amendment to definition of income SB 1144 would exclude from the definition of income an individual's pro rata share of income of any bank or other financial institution in which the individual has a 10% interest or greater. The practical effect of the legislation is to make transaction between a bank, in which a public official has a significant ownership interest (10% or greater), and the bank's customer unreportable. In addition, the bank's customer would not be a source of income to the official for disqualification purposes. Presently, the Act provides that a public official, with a 10% interest or greater in a bank, must list the name of a bank customer, if the official's pro rata share of the gross receipts is $10,000 or more. It is current Commission policy that deposits (savings and checking accounts) are not gross receipts and individuals who only maintain accounts need not be reported. I have rendered advice saying that people who repay loans to banks are bank customers. If the filer's pro rata share of the repayments is $10,000 or more, the customer's name should be disclosed. I suspect that this advice was the impetus for this proposed legislation. DED:nrw
embedding.summary: This internal FPPC memorandum from May 1979 addresses the treatment of bank loan repayments as gross receipts under California's political practices disclosure law and discusses staff opposition to SB 1144, which would have excluded bank customer transactions from reportable income.
